System Design (BASIC):
**System Context:** An e-commerce startup with 10K daily active users needs a notification system. Currently all notifications (order confirmations, shipping updates, promotional alerts) are sent synchronously during API request handling, causing 3-5 second response times on the order placement endpoint. The backend is a single Python FastAPI service with PostgreSQL.

**Design Challenge:** Design an asynchronous notification service that supports email, SMS, and push notifications. The candidate should define the component architecture, message flow, and API contract for triggering notifications from the main application.

**Constraints:** Must handle at least 3 notification channels without blocking the main API. Use a message queue (candidate chooses which). Budget for 1-2 additional services. Peak load: 500 notifications/minute. Must support notification preferences (users opt out of certain channels). 99% of notifications must be delivered within 5 minutes.

**Expected Sections:** Component Architecture (main app + notification service + queue), API Contract (trigger notification endpoint), Data Model (notification records + user preferences), Message Queue Choice with justification, Failure Handling (what if SMS provider is down?).

**System Context:** A SaaS analytics company needs a URL shortener feature for their marketing team. Short links will be embedded in email campaigns to track click-through rates. Expected usage: 50K short URLs created/month, 500K clicks/month. The company already runs a Node.js backend with MongoDB.

**Design Challenge:** Design the URL shortener service including the shortening algorithm, redirect flow, and click analytics tracking. The candidate should propose how to generate unique short codes, handle redirects with minimal latency, and store click event data for analytics dashboards.

**Constraints:** Short codes should be 6-8 characters (alphanumeric). Redirect latency must be under 100ms for p99. Must track: click count, referrer URL, timestamp, country (from IP). Must handle duplicate URL submissions gracefully. Single service deployment. Data retention: click data kept for 1 year.

**Expected Sections:** Short Code Generation Strategy (algorithm + uniqueness guarantee), Redirect Flow (sequence diagram from click to destination), Data Model (URLs table + click events), API Endpoints (create, redirect, get analytics), Caching Strategy for hot URLs.

**System Context:** A fitness app startup has 5K users tracking workouts. The current system stores all workout data in a single PostgreSQL table with 2M rows. The mobile app fetches workout history via a REST API, but users are reporting slow load times (2-3 seconds) for their workout history page, which shows weekly/monthly aggregates and personal bests.

**Design Challenge:** Design a workout analytics service that pre-computes user statistics (weekly totals, monthly trends, personal records) and serves them with sub-200ms latency. The candidate should decide where to compute aggregates, how to store them, and how to keep them fresh when new workouts are logged.

**Constraints:** Users log 1-3 workouts/day. Analytics page must load in under 200ms. Aggregates include: weekly volume, monthly frequency, personal bests per exercise, streak tracking. Budget: 1 additional service + 1 cache layer. Must handle late data entry (user logs yesterday's workout today).

**Expected Sections:** Component Architecture (API + aggregation layer + storage), Aggregation Strategy (real-time vs batch vs hybrid), Data Model (raw workouts vs pre-computed stats), Cache Design (what to cache, invalidation strategy), API Contract (analytics endpoints).

**System Context:** A small B2B invoicing platform has 500 businesses as clients. Currently, invoices are generated as HTML and emailed directly from the API request handler. The system needs to support PDF generation, scheduled recurring invoices, and invoice status tracking (draft, sent, viewed, paid). The backend is a Django application with PostgreSQL.

**Design Challenge:** Design the invoice lifecycle management system that handles invoice creation, PDF generation, scheduled sending, and status tracking. The candidate should define how recurring invoices are triggered, how PDF generation is handled without blocking the API, and how invoice status transitions work.

**Constraints:** PDF generation takes 2-5 seconds per invoice. Recurring invoices must be sent within a 1-hour window of their scheduled time. Must support: draft/sent/viewed/paid status with audit trail. Monthly invoice volume: ~10K. Must handle bulk invoice generation (end-of-month for all clients). Single application deployment.

**Expected Sections:** Component Architecture (API + background worker + scheduler), Invoice State Machine (status transitions with rules), Data Model (invoices + recurrence rules + status history), Background Processing Strategy (PDF generation + email sending), Scheduling Design (how recurring invoices are triggered).

**System Context:** A property listing website has 20K listings and 50K monthly visitors. The search functionality currently does full-table SQL queries with LIKE clauses, resulting in 1-2 second search times. Users search by location, price range, number of bedrooms, and free-text keywords (e.g., 'garden', 'parking'). The backend is a Rails application with PostgreSQL.

**Design Challenge:** Design a search system that supports multi-criteria filtering with free-text search and returns results in under 200ms. The candidate should decide on the search infrastructure, how listing data flows from the main database to the search layer, and how search results stay in sync when listings are created/updated/deleted.

**Constraints:** 20K listings, growing 500/month. Search must combine: location (city/area), price range, bedrooms, free-text keywords. Results must reflect listing changes within 5 minutes. Must support sort by: relevance, price (asc/desc), date listed. Budget: 1 search service. Must not impact the main application's write performance.

**Expected Sections:** Search Infrastructure Choice (with justification), Data Sync Strategy (how listings get into the search index), API Contract (search endpoint with filters/sort/pagination), Index Design (what fields, how text is analyzed), Trade-offs (search engine vs enhanced SQL).
Java (INTERMEDIATE):
**System Context:** An e-commerce platform's Java order service handles 100K orders/day. The service currently processes order fulfillment synchronously: inventory reservation, warehouse assignment, shipping label generation, and customer notification — all in one Spring Boot request thread. During flash sales, the service hits thread pool exhaustion (Tomcat default 200 threads all blocked on downstream calls), causing cascading failures. Current stack: Spring Boot 2.7, Spring MVC, JPA with PostgreSQL, RestTemplate for service calls.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the order fulfillment pipeline to handle flash sale spikes (10x normal load) without thread pool exhaustion. The candidate should decide between reactive (WebFlux) vs async (CompletableFuture + thread pools) vs event-driven approaches, design the concurrency strategy, and define how to handle partial failures in the fulfillment pipeline.

**Constraints:** Must handle 10x spike: 1000 orders/minute during flash sales. Current downstream services: inventory (p99=200ms), warehouse (p99=500ms), shipping (p99=1s), notification (p99=300ms). JVM heap budget: 2GB per service instance. Must preserve order consistency — inventory must be reserved before warehouse assignment. Max 3 service instances during peak. Must gracefully degrade — if shipping label generation is slow, don't block inventory reservation.

**Expected Sections:** Concurrency & Threading Strategy (reactive vs async vs event-driven with justification), Service Architecture, Framework & Library Choices (Spring MVC vs WebFlux, thread pool config), Failure Handling & Resilience (partial fulfillment, compensating actions), Data Model & Storage, Deployment & Scaling (JVM tuning for flash sales), Trade-offs & Alternatives.

**System Context:** A healthcare SaaS company runs a Spring Boot appointment scheduling system used by 200 clinics. The system handles: appointment booking, doctor availability management, patient reminders (email + SMS), and waitlist management. Currently, checking doctor availability involves multiple JPA queries with N+1 problems, causing 3-second response times on the booking page. The reminder system runs as a @Scheduled cron job that queries all upcoming appointments every 5 minutes, causing database load spikes. Current stack: Spring Boot 3.1, Spring Data JPA, PostgreSQL, Quartz scheduler.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the appointment system to solve the availability query performance issue and replace the polling-based reminder system with an event-driven approach. The candidate should design a caching strategy for doctor availability, an event-driven reminder pipeline, and define how the waitlist is managed when cancellations occur.

**Constraints:** 200 clinics, ~50 doctors per clinic, 15-minute appointment slots. Availability must reflect booking changes within 10 seconds. Reminders must be sent: 24h before, 2h before, and 15min before appointment. Must handle: double-booking prevention, timezone-aware scheduling across clinics, waitlist auto-promotion when a slot opens. Database connection pool: 20 connections. Must not increase p99 booking latency beyond 500ms.

**Expected Sections:** Data Model & Storage (availability representation, cache design), Caching Strategy (what to cache, invalidation on booking), Event/Message Flow (booking events -> reminders, cancellation -> waitlist), Concurrency & Threading (preventing double-booking, cache consistency), Framework & Library Choices (caching library, event mechanism), API Design (booking + availability endpoints), Failure Handling (what if reminder service is down?).

**System Context:** A logistics company tracks 10K active shipments in real-time. GPS devices on delivery trucks send location updates every 30 seconds via HTTP POST to a Spring Boot ingestion service. The current implementation writes each update directly to PostgreSQL, then the tracking dashboard polls the database every 5 seconds for the latest positions. During peak hours (5K trucks active), the database becomes a bottleneck — write latency spikes to 2 seconds, and the dashboard shows stale data. Current stack: Spring Boot 2.7, JPA, PostgreSQL, no caching, no streaming.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the location tracking pipeline to handle high-throughput GPS ingestion, provide real-time position updates to the dashboard, and support geofence alerting (notify when a truck enters/leaves a defined area). The candidate should design the ingestion pipeline, choose appropriate storage for time-series location data, and define how real-time updates reach the dashboard.

**Constraints:** 10K trucks, GPS update every 30s = ~333 updates/second sustained, bursts up to 500/second. Dashboard must show positions updated within 5 seconds of GPS ping. Must store 30 days of location history for route replay. Geofence alerts must trigger within 60 seconds. Budget: 2-3 Java services max. Must support 500 concurrent dashboard users. JVM heap: 4GB per service.

**Expected Sections:** Service Architecture & Boundaries (ingestion, storage, real-time, alerting), Data Model & Storage (time-series storage choice, hot vs cold data), Ingestion Pipeline (batching, back-pressure, thread pool design), Real-time Update Strategy (WebSocket/SSE, how updates flow from ingestion to dashboard), Geofence Processing (how to efficiently check geofences), Framework & Library Choices (Spring WebFlux vs MVC for ingestion, streaming libraries), Deployment & Scaling.

**System Context:** A mid-size bank runs a Java-based loan application processing system. Loan applications go through multiple stages: document upload, credit score check (external API, 5-10s response), manual underwriter review (async, hours-days), approval/rejection, and disbursement. Currently this is a Spring Boot monolith where application state is tracked via JPA entity status fields, background processing uses @Async with a SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor (no proper thread pool), and the underwriter UI polls the database for new applications. When the credit score API is slow, all @Async threads are blocked, starving the underwriter queue. Current stack: Spring Boot 3.0, Spring Data JPA, PostgreSQL, no message broker.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the loan processing pipeline as a workflow engine with proper state management, bounded async processing, and resilient external API integration. The candidate should define the workflow stages, choose between orchestration (workflow engine) vs choreography (events), and design the system to handle long-running processes (underwriter review can take days).

**Constraints:** 500 loan applications/day, 50 concurrent in-flight applications. Credit score API: p99=10s, 5% failure rate, costs $0.50/call (no unnecessary retries). Underwriter review SLA: must be assigned within 1 hour of credit check completion. Must support: application state visibility (where is my application?), resumability (if service restarts, in-flight applications resume), audit trail (every state transition logged). Must use Java/Spring ecosystem.

**Expected Sections:** Workflow Architecture (orchestration vs choreography choice with justification), State Machine Design (loan application states + valid transitions), Service Architecture & Boundaries, Framework & Library Choices (Spring State Machine vs Temporal vs custom, thread pool config), External API Integration (credit score — resilience, caching, cost optimization), Data Model & Storage (application state, audit trail), Failure Handling & Resilience (service restart recovery, stuck application detection), Observability & Monitoring.
SQL (INTERMEDIATE):
**System Context:** A retail analytics company runs a PostgreSQL data warehouse with 500M+ rows across fact and dimension tables. The current system uses a single unpartitioned fact_sales table, nightly full-refresh ETL jobs that take 6+ hours, and analysts query the warehouse directly during business hours causing resource contention. Materialized views are absent — every dashboard query re-computes aggregations from raw data. The analytics team (15 analysts) reports query timeouts during morning hours when ETL jobs overlap with dashboard usage. Current stack: PostgreSQL 15, single primary instance, no read replicas, PgBouncer with 50 connections, cron-based ETL scripts.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the data warehouse architecture to support concurrent ETL and analytical workloads without resource contention. The candidate should design a partitioning strategy for the fact table, a materialized view layer for dashboard queries, an incremental ETL pipeline to replace the full-refresh approach, and a read replica topology to separate analytical and ETL workloads.

**Constraints:** Fact table: 500M rows, growing 2M/day. 15 concurrent analysts during business hours. ETL must complete within a 2-hour nightly window. Dashboard queries must return in under 3 seconds (p95). Budget: 1 primary + 2 read replicas. Must support 90-day hot data, 2-year warm data, 5-year cold archive. Connection pool limit: 100 connections across all replicas. Zero-downtime migration from current schema.

**Expected Sections:** Schema Architecture (partitioning strategy, fact/dimension redesign), Materialized View Layer (which aggregations, refresh strategy), ETL Pipeline Redesign (incremental CDC, staging tables, validation), Read Replica Topology (routing analytics vs ETL), Indexing Strategy (what indexes on partitioned tables), Data Lifecycle Management (hot/warm/cold tiers, archival), Migration Plan (zero-downtime from current to new schema), Monitoring & Observability (pg_stat_statements, replication lag, ETL health).

**System Context:** A fintech company processes 200K financial transactions/day through a PostgreSQL OLTP database. The system needs to generate real-time compliance reports (anti-money laundering, suspicious activity) while maintaining sub-100ms transaction processing latency. Currently, compliance queries run directly against the OLTP tables, causing lock contention and degrading transaction processing. The compliance team needs rolling 90-day windows, cross-account pattern detection, and threshold-based alerting. Current stack: PostgreSQL 14, single instance, no partitioning, basic B-tree indexes, application-level connection pooling (20 connections).

**Design Challenge:** Design a database architecture that separates OLTP transaction processing from OLAP compliance reporting without data staleness exceeding 5 minutes. The candidate should design the schema for both workloads, a CDC pipeline to feed compliance data, partitioning for the compliance reporting tables, and stored procedures for pattern detection.

**Constraints:** Transaction table: 200K inserts/day, p99 insert latency <100ms. Compliance reports must reflect data no older than 5 minutes. Pattern detection queries span 90-day windows across 50K accounts. Must support 10 concurrent compliance analysts. Compliance data retention: 7 years. Must handle schema evolution without OLTP downtime. Budget: 2 PostgreSQL instances. Must pass SOC2 audit (row-level access control, audit trail on all compliance queries).

**Expected Sections:** Dual-Database Architecture (OLTP vs OLAP separation), CDC Pipeline Design (logical replication vs trigger-based), Compliance Schema Design (partitioning by date, indexing for pattern queries), Stored Procedure Design (rolling window aggregations, threshold alerting), Transaction Isolation Strategy (OLTP isolation level, read consistency for reports), Security & Access Control (row-level security, audit logging), Data Retention & Archival (7-year compliance archive), Migration & Rollout Plan.

**System Context:** A healthcare data platform aggregates patient records from 50 hospital systems into a central PostgreSQL database for population health analytics. Each hospital pushes data in different formats (HL7, FHIR, CSV) at varying frequencies (real-time, hourly, daily). The current system loads all data into a single staging table, then a monolithic stored procedure transforms and loads it into normalized tables — this procedure takes 4+ hours and frequently fails partway through, requiring manual intervention. The normalized schema has grown to 30+ tables with complex foreign key relationships, making bulk loads extremely slow. Current stack: PostgreSQL 13, single instance, 200GB database, no partitioning, basic cron scheduling.

**Design Challenge:** Design a robust multi-source data ingestion and transformation pipeline that handles heterogeneous hospital data feeds, supports incremental loading, and maintains referential integrity across the normalized schema. The candidate should design the staging layer, transformation logic partitioning, error handling strategy, and data quality validation framework.

**Constraints:** 50 data sources with 3 different formats. Daily data volume: 5GB across all sources. Transformation must complete within 1 hour for each source. Must handle: partial failures (source A fails, source B succeeds), schema evolution (hospitals add fields), duplicate detection across sources. Referential integrity must be maintained — no orphaned records. Must support point-in-time queries for audit (what did the data look like on date X?). Budget: 1 primary + 1 replica.

**Expected Sections:** Ingestion Architecture (multi-format parsing, source-specific staging), Staging Schema Design (raw vs validated vs transformed layers), Transformation Pipeline (incremental, idempotent, resumable), Data Quality Framework (validation rules, error quarantine, reconciliation), Schema Design (SCD Type 2 for patient records, temporal queries), Indexing & Partitioning Strategy, Error Handling & Recovery (partial failure isolation), Monitoring & Alerting (pipeline health, data freshness, quality metrics).

**System Context:** An e-commerce marketplace with 10M product listings uses PostgreSQL for its product catalog and search. The current system stores all product data in a single products table with 50+ columns (attributes vary by category — electronics have specs, clothing has sizes/colors, furniture has dimensions). Search queries use LIKE clauses and multiple JOINs across attribute tables, resulting in 2-5 second response times. Category-specific filtering is slow because attribute data is stored in an EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value) pattern. The product team wants to add faceted search, dynamic filtering by category-specific attributes, and real-time inventory counts. Current stack: PostgreSQL 15, EAV schema, no full-text search indexes, basic B-tree indexes on product_id only.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the product catalog schema to support efficient category-specific filtering, faceted search, and real-time inventory integration. The candidate should design a schema that handles heterogeneous product attributes without EAV overhead, a search/filtering strategy using PostgreSQL capabilities (GIN indexes, JSONB, full-text search), and an inventory sync mechanism.

**Constraints:** 10M products across 500 categories. Each category has 5-20 unique filterable attributes. Search must return results in under 500ms (p95). Must support: faceted counts (how many products match each filter value), sort by price/relevance/rating/newest. Inventory updates arrive every 30 seconds from warehouse systems. Must handle 1000 concurrent search requests. Budget: 2 PostgreSQL instances (primary + read replica). Must migrate from EAV without downtime.

**Expected Sections:** Schema Redesign (JSONB vs wide tables vs table-per-category trade-offs), Indexing Strategy (GIN on JSONB, full-text search with tsvector, expression indexes for facets), Search & Filtering Design (how faceted counts are computed efficiently), Inventory Sync Architecture (real-time vs near-real-time, cache layer), Query Optimization (materialized views for popular facets, connection pooling), Read Replica Strategy (routing search vs writes), Migration Plan (EAV to new schema with zero downtime), Monitoring (slow query detection, index usage tracking).

**System Context:** A SaaS platform serves 500 tenant organizations, each with their own data isolation requirements. The current PostgreSQL setup uses a shared-schema approach with a tenant_id column on every table, but as the platform has grown to 100M+ rows, cross-tenant query interference has become a problem — one tenant's large export job slows down all other tenants. The platform needs to support tenant-specific backup/restore, per-tenant data retention policies, and the ability to migrate large tenants to dedicated infrastructure. Current stack: PostgreSQL 14, shared schema with tenant_id, no row-level security, single database, PgBouncer.

**Design Challenge:** Design a multi-tenant database architecture that provides data isolation, prevents cross-tenant performance interference, and supports tenant migration to dedicated infrastructure. The candidate should evaluate shared-schema vs schema-per-tenant vs database-per-tenant trade-offs, design the routing layer, and plan the migration path from the current shared-schema approach.

**Constraints:** 500 tenants, ranging from 10K to 50M rows each. Top 10 tenants account for 80% of data and query volume. Must support per-tenant backup/restore without affecting other tenants. Cross-tenant query interference must be eliminated. Connection pool budget: 200 connections total. Must support tenant onboarding (new tenant setup in under 5 minutes) and offboarding (data deletion within 24 hours). Large tenant migration to dedicated instance must happen with under 1 hour downtime.

**Expected Sections:** Multi-Tenancy Architecture (shared vs schema-per-tenant vs hybrid — trade-offs and recommendation), Data Isolation Strategy (row-level security, schema separation, connection routing), Partitioning by Tenant (how to partition large shared tables), Connection Pooling & Routing (PgBouncer config, tenant-aware routing), Backup & Restore Strategy (per-tenant granularity), Tenant Migration Plan (shared to dedicated with minimal downtime), Performance Isolation (resource limits, query timeouts per tenant), Monitoring & Observability (per-tenant metrics, noisy neighbor detection).
Python FastAPI (INTERMEDIATE):
**System Context:** A media streaming company runs a FastAPI monolith that handles user authentication, content catalog, recommendation engine, and streaming session management. The service processes 50K API requests/minute during peak hours. The recommendation endpoint calls an external ML inference service (p99=2s) synchronously, blocking the event loop and causing all other endpoints to degrade. Background tasks for analytics event processing use FastAPI BackgroundTasks but lose events on service restarts. Current stack: FastAPI 0.104, sync SQLAlchemy with PostgreSQL, FastAPI BackgroundTasks, no message broker, single Uvicorn worker, no caching.

**Design Challenge:** Decompose the monolith into bounded FastAPI services and redesign the async processing pipeline. The candidate must define service boundaries, choose between Celery/ARQ/Dramatiq for reliable background processing, design a caching layer for the content catalog, and solve the blocking ML inference call without degrading other endpoints.

**Constraints:** Must handle 50K requests/minute at peak. Recommendation endpoint: must return in <500ms (with fallback if ML service is slow). Analytics events: zero event loss, even during deployments. Content catalog: 100K items, updates 100 times/day, reads 10K times/minute. Must use Python/FastAPI ecosystem. Budget: 3-4 services max. Container resources: 2 CPU, 4GB RAM per service. Must support graceful deployment (no dropped requests during rolling update).

**Expected Sections:** Service Architecture & Boundaries (auth, catalog, recommendations, analytics), Async Strategy (how to handle blocking ML call — thread pool executor, separate async service, or event-driven), Background Processing (Celery vs ARQ vs Dramatiq with justification, broker choice), Caching Design (Redis strategy for catalog, invalidation on updates), API Design (inter-service communication, API gateway), Data Model & Storage (per-service databases, shared data strategy), Failure Handling & Resilience (ML service timeout/fallback, circuit breaker with tenacity), Deployment & Scaling (Uvicorn worker config, container sizing, graceful shutdown), Observability (structlog, OpenTelemetry, Prometheus).

**System Context:** An IoT platform ingests sensor data from 50K devices, each sending readings every 10 seconds. A FastAPI ingestion service receives HTTP POST requests and writes directly to PostgreSQL — during peak hours, write latency exceeds 5 seconds, and the database connection pool is exhausted. A separate FastAPI dashboard service queries the same database for real-time visualizations and historical trends, but analytical queries on the growing time-series data (500M+ rows) cause table locks that further degrade ingestion. Current stack: FastAPI 0.109, async SQLAlchemy 2.0, PostgreSQL, no message broker, no caching, 4 Uvicorn workers.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the IoT data platform to handle high-throughput ingestion, separate real-time queries from historical analytics, and support alerting when sensor readings breach configured thresholds. The candidate should design the ingestion pipeline with batching/buffering, choose appropriate storage for time-series data, and define how real-time and historical query workloads are isolated.

**Constraints:** 50K devices × 1 reading/10s = 5K writes/second sustained. Real-time dashboard must show data within 15 seconds of device transmission. Historical queries span 30-90 day windows. Must support threshold-based alerts (e.g., temperature > 80°C) with <60 second detection. Data retention: 1 year hot, 5 years archive. Budget: 3 FastAPI services max. Must handle device reconnection storms (10K devices reconnecting simultaneously after network outage). Connection pool: 50 connections per service.

**Expected Sections:** Service Architecture (ingestion, real-time query, historical analytics, alerting), Ingestion Pipeline Design (batching with asyncio, back-pressure handling, write buffering), Storage Strategy (PostgreSQL with TimescaleDB vs InfluxDB vs partitioned PostgreSQL — trade-offs), Real-time vs Historical Query Separation (CQRS pattern, read replicas), Alerting Pipeline (stream processing vs polling, threshold evaluation), Async Design (event loop management, blocking I/O isolation), Framework Choices (FastAPI + aiokafka, async SQLAlchemy, aioredis), Deployment & Scaling (Uvicorn/Gunicorn config, horizontal scaling), Failure Handling (device reconnection storms, ingestion back-pressure).

**System Context:** A food delivery platform runs three FastAPI services: order management, restaurant integration, and delivery tracking. The order service coordinates the flow: customer places order → restaurant confirms → driver assigned → delivery tracked → order completed. Currently, services communicate via synchronous REST calls in a chain — the order service calls restaurant service, waits for confirmation, then calls delivery service. If any downstream service is slow or down, the entire order flow fails. There is no retry mechanism, no event history, and failed orders require manual intervention. Current stack: FastAPI 0.100, sync httpx for service calls, PostgreSQL per service, no message broker, no circuit breakers.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the order orchestration flow using event-driven architecture with proper saga pattern implementation. The candidate should define event schemas, choose between orchestration (central coordinator) vs choreography (each service reacts to events), design compensating actions for failures (restaurant confirmed but no driver available), and ensure exactly-once processing semantics.

**Constraints:** 10K orders/day, peak 500 orders/hour during dinner rush. Order placement to restaurant confirmation: must complete in <30 seconds. Restaurant confirmation to driver assignment: must complete in <2 minutes. Must handle: restaurant rejection after confirmation, driver cancellation after assignment, payment refunds on cancellation. Must support order status tracking (customer sees real-time updates). Must use Python ecosystem. Event history must be retained for 90 days for dispute resolution. Budget: existing 3 services + 1 message broker.

**Expected Sections:** Event-Driven Architecture (orchestration vs choreography with justification), Saga Pattern Design (order lifecycle states, compensating actions for each failure scenario), Event Schema Design (order events, versioning strategy), Message Broker Choice (Kafka vs RabbitMQ for Python ecosystem — aiokafka vs aio-pika), Service Communication (async event handling, idempotent consumers), Data Model (event store, order state, saga state), Failure Handling (dead letter queues, stuck saga detection, manual intervention API), Real-time Status Updates (WebSocket/SSE from FastAPI), Observability (distributed tracing across services, saga monitoring).

**System Context:** A B2B document processing platform receives PDF/Word documents via API upload, extracts text using OCR (external service, 10-30s per document), classifies documents using an ML model, extracts structured data (invoice fields, contract terms), and stores results. The current FastAPI service handles everything synchronously — upload, OCR, classification, extraction, storage — in a single request that times out for large documents. The service runs with 4 Uvicorn workers, and during batch uploads (customers upload 100+ documents at once), all workers are blocked on OCR calls, making the service unresponsive. Current stack: FastAPI 0.105, Celery with Redis broker (but poorly configured — no task routing, no rate limiting, tasks retry infinitely on failure), PostgreSQL, S3 for document storage.

**Design Challenge:** Redesign the document processing pipeline with proper async task orchestration, stage-based processing, and resilient external service integration. The candidate should design the task workflow (upload → OCR → classify → extract → store), implement proper Celery task chains with error handling, design rate limiting for the OCR service, and ensure the upload endpoint returns immediately with a processing status URL.

**Constraints:** Peak: 500 document uploads/hour, batch uploads of 100+ documents per customer. OCR service: p99=30s, rate limit 20 concurrent requests, 2% failure rate. ML classification: p99=5s, runs on GPU with 10 concurrent request limit. Must support: processing status tracking per document, partial failure handling (OCR succeeds but classification fails — don't re-OCR), batch progress reporting. Document sizes: 1-50MB. Must complete processing within 15 minutes per document. Budget: existing infrastructure + properly configured task queues.

**Expected Sections:** Pipeline Architecture (stage-based processing with status tracking), Task Queue Design (Celery task chains/chords, routing, priority queues), Rate Limiting Strategy (OCR and ML service concurrency limits, token bucket), Upload API Design (immediate response with status URL, batch upload support), Error Handling & Retry (stage-specific retry policies, dead letter handling, no infinite retries), Storage Strategy (S3 for documents, PostgreSQL for metadata and status), Monitoring & Observability (task queue metrics, processing latency tracking, failure alerting), Deployment (Celery worker scaling, memory management for large documents).

**System Context:** A multi-tenant SaaS platform provides analytics dashboards to 200 enterprise customers. Each customer's data is in a shared PostgreSQL database with tenant_id-based isolation. The FastAPI backend serves both a real-time API (dashboard data refreshing every 30 seconds) and a report generation API (PDF/Excel exports that take 30-120 seconds). During peak hours, report generation tasks consume all database connections (pool size: 30), causing real-time dashboard requests to queue and timeout. The platform also needs to support webhook notifications to customer systems when specific metric thresholds are breached. Current stack: FastAPI 0.108, async SQLAlchemy 2.0, PostgreSQL (shared schema), Redis for session storage only, no background task system, 8 Uvicorn workers.

**Design Challenge:** Design a multi-tenant architecture that isolates real-time API workloads from heavy report generation, implements webhook delivery with retry logic, and prevents noisy-neighbor problems where one tenant's heavy usage degrades performance for others. The candidate should design the connection pool strategy, report generation pipeline, webhook delivery system, and per-tenant rate limiting.

**Constraints:** 200 tenants, top 20 account for 70% of query volume. Real-time dashboard: must respond in <500ms (p95). Report generation: up to 50 concurrent reports, each scanning 1-10M rows. Webhook delivery: must attempt delivery within 60 seconds of threshold breach, retry 3 times with exponential backoff. Must support per-tenant rate limiting (100 API requests/minute for standard tier, 1000 for enterprise). Must handle: tenant data isolation (no cross-tenant data leaks), connection pool exhaustion prevention, graceful degradation under load. Budget: 2-3 FastAPI services + Redis + task queue.

**Expected Sections:** Multi-Tenant Architecture (connection routing, tenant isolation, noisy neighbor prevention), Service Separation (real-time API vs report generation vs webhook delivery), Connection Pool Strategy (separate pools for real-time vs batch, per-tenant limits), Report Generation Pipeline (background task system, progress tracking, result caching), Webhook Delivery System (reliable delivery, retry with backoff, delivery logging), Rate Limiting Design (per-tenant limits, slowapi or custom middleware), Caching Strategy (tenant-aware Redis caching, dashboard data pre-computation), Deployment & Scaling (Uvicorn worker tuning, horizontal scaling strategy), Monitoring (per-tenant metrics, connection pool health, webhook delivery SLA tracking).
